title: Dog Bleeding from Vulva (Not in Heat): Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Heavy vaginal bleeding in dogs not in heat is a high-priority emergency. Learn the potential causes and why immediate veterinary care is critical.
content_html:
What is Heavy Vaginal Bleeding?

Heavy bleeding from the vulva that occurs outside of a dog's regular heat cycle (estrus) is an abnormal clinical sign. While light spotting is normal during heat, active hemorrhage or heavy dripping indicates a potentially serious underlying health issue. This bleeding may originate from the uterus, the vaginal wall, or even the urinary tract, and it requires professional assessment to determine the source.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, heavy bleeding from the vulva when a dog is not in heat is a medical emergency. This symptom can be a sign of life-threatening conditions such as pyometra (a severe uterine infection), reproductive tract tumors, significant internal trauma, or blood clotting disorders. If your dog also appears lethargic, has pale gums, or is panting excessively, seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so, taking a clear photograph of the bleeding and any discharge can be invaluable for the triage process. A photo allows the veterinary team to evaluate the color, volume, and consistency of the blood before you arrive at the clinic. This visual information helps the medical staff prepare the necessary diagnostic tools and prioritize your dog’s treatment based on the severity of the blood loss.

faqs.question: Can a uterine infection cause bleeding?
faqs.answer: Yes, an 'open' pyometra occurs when the cervix is open, allowing pus and blood to drain from the infected uterus. This is a life-threatening condition requiring immediate surgery.

faqs.question: Could this be a Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)?
faqs.answer: While a severe UTI or bladder stones can cause blood in the urine, heavy or continuous dripping is more commonly associated with the reproductive tract and requires urgent differentiation by a vet.

faqs.question: What should I do on the way to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog calm and restricted in a crate or small area. Do not attempt to clean the area or insert anything into the vagina, as this can cause further trauma or mask diagnostic signs.
